speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe you could just continue telling me what happened when you went-- what did you do in Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my husband was in veneer business and in plywood. And it was a very-- they were the golden years. From 1960 to '70, was really-- they were good times there. And we lived a good life there. And mainly I was entertaining and business people when they came. I would-- we would go out, take them to restaurants and entertaining, because that was the business. When they came, you had to take them out to eat. And I made lots of friends that way as well, through business. And all these things were going down. And my husband got very ill. He had kidney trouble. And he had to be on dialysis. And we had to come back as well, first of all because business went bad. And then he had an operation in London at the London clinic. And this went wrong. It was very, very traumatic time. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were your children by then? When were they born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter was born in 1949. And my son is 1952.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Denise and Michael. Denise Francis and Michael George. And well they have got-- Denise's husband is Eliezer Kienwald. He is from Italy. She met him while we were in Italy. He was born in Rome, but the parents moved to Milan when he was two and a half years old. And my, my son married a girl. She's Diane [? Asher. ?] She was born in England. But as a small baby, they went to South Africa. They came back a few years ago. When trouble started in South Africa and so on, they came back here. My son married-- is married to her. And he's got two children-- Maya Lauren and Julien. My daughter has got four children. She's got Sharon, Doniel, Aron, and Kochawa, which is a star in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let's go back to the period when you came back from Italy. What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we came back and things went really drastically wrong. And my husband got very ill. And the operations didn't go right for him. And he died. It was a terrible, traumatic time. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] things didn't work out at all well at the end anyway. It was very bad. And then I came to England. We came to England. And I had to start fresh. And I started working. At first, I actually worked as a chemist for a couple of years. And then I went to work in corsetry. But in corsetry, I only worked part time. I managed all right because I am experienced corsetier. So I had a good job there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Golders Green, at Madame Leiberg's. She took me with both her hands. She used to-- when I first got married, and I was a living in, in Haverstock Hill. I lived together with my mother-in-law at one time, right in beginning. And I had this sewing machine. And when I had my first baby, and I couldn't go out right in the beginning, I used to go to Mrs. Leiberg and collect one, two dozen bras. And I used to make them at home for pin money-- to have extra money. And I used to bring it back to Mrs. Leiberg. She used to know me very well. Because I could make these things. I learned corsetry from the beginning. So she gave me cotton. I made it up. And when the time came, and I came to England, and she heard that I am now available, she was very pleased to have me, because she couldn't get people who actually knew corsetry. Some people she had who maybe could sell, but not actually fit properly and adjust whatever needed doing. Well anyway, whilst I was at Madame Leiberg's also my daughter got married. My daughter got married in September the 3rd. And by the time, me having lived such an easy, good life, suddenly to have to work and in preparation of the wedding and everything-- it was getting so much for me. I was getting very tired. And well, one day, I, I heard that there is a group-- a Jewish group-- going to, to Majorca, only for a prolonged weekend-- four days, three nights. And I said, oh, that would do me good. i mean, not that I would choose exactly Majorca, but it's a nice place. But it was so easy to get to Majorca in those days. It was so very cheap in those days. The whole trip was 22 pounds, but when you think about it now, in those days probably was also an amount. But it was still not expensive even in those days. It was the flight. It was the hotel. It was everything. And when I came to this-- when it came to actually going, that morning I overslept. I always-- whenever I go somewhere, can't sleep all night. But here, I slept in the morning. I must have been up all night. And I said, that's it. I won't be able to go anymore. Anyway, my son was here with me at the time. And I said, Michael-- I had to know at the door to tell him because I wouldn't wake him normally-- Michael, I wouldn't be able to go to Majorca. He jumped out of bed like a crazy one. He did everything. He drove me there. And he-- and I arrived. I was the last one to arrive. But when I came, the organizer-- at first, it was supposed to be a group of about 25. It turned out only it was eight with me. And the eight was one couple, and the others were all single people. So there were six single people. So this, this man, Norman comes along with Peter, and he introduces me. This is Minia and Peter. Peter took my little suitcase. And from then onwards, we were sitting on the train together. We were talking together. We went on the plane together. And the three nights-- we were always, all the girls were together. But we were like a group, always going out together. But when it came to evening dancing, we were dancing together. And when we came from this weekend, it turned out that he lived only around the corner from me-- walking distance. So right in the beginning-- I-- when we came back, we lost a bit of contact. We didn't really-- I went to work. I was Tuesdays working at the chemist still.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- one day, he comes to the chemist where I am. I was so surprised. I said, how come you came to this chemist here? He said, well my doctor's right here. So it means, he lived here. But his doctor was in Regent's Park Road, right near next to the chemist where I was. Unbelievable. He came to fill a prescription. And from then always, we started seeing each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he did phone me several times, which I didn't know. My son took the telephone calls. And he said, Mommy, a man is phoning here but he's not leaving a name. And that was him. I didn't know. So after that, I found that when he had Michael-- my son has got a manly voice-- so he thought it was a boyfriend or something. He didn't think it could be son. And this is how it was that he-- and then we met up together, and we started going out. And we went out for four and a half years. And I was helping him in the business. And I was working later on in the corset place.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the 25 years which I didn't work, I gave every moment back of work. I did-- I worked very, very hard. And that was the story. We go married four and half years later. Which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a bachelor. This year is 20 years, exactly. He was a bachelor, and I was a grandmother. I had my granddaughter on my lap when I got married. Now, is that a story, no? He-- because-- I mean, would we have married before, it would have been different. But we were waiting and waiting, and four and a half years-- in the meantime, my daughter had this little girl, Sharon. She'll be here soon. This little girl is 23 now. And she is-- she was on my lap. She was two and a half years old when we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me, throughout that time, did you stay in touch with the other boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Always. We always have got this bond that we are always-- we, we are like brothers and-- even if we don't see each other. But we are brothers and sisters. And we, every year, we've got a reunion. Then we have got, during the year also meetings-- or there's a Hanukkah party or there is another-- we always get together. We always meet, yes. We some are more in contact than others. But I see them all when there is a reunion.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you know most of them personally or, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I know them. I remember them each one when they came over. They remember me. I mean we were one family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there-- is it mostly the people who came from Prague or the all the other children as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Our, our, our transport was from Poland. All of them are mainly-- our transport is from Poland. The next transport came already from Hungary, Czechoslovakia, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, sorry, I meant from Prague-- from the place, Prague.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, yeah. We came over from Theresien, where we were liberated. But the extra people are from Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. But are you closer to the people in that-- from that group?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I am also of the second group. I also know them very well. It's so many years. We are also very-- look, we all got the same story practically to tell. So we are very close. But with the boys I came over on the 45. You see, it's a different story because we actually came over together in the same transport. We were in Czechoslo-- Theresien, together in Czechoslovakia. We were together in Prague. We were together in Windermere. Different story. I mean when they call themselves now, one is called-- I know them by the first name. One was [NON-ENGLISH], Monty. One was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] so, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] so if we, we-- I call them exactly the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Can you tell me, when was it the first time the name 'the boys' was used actually? How did this name come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: The boys are because there were boys and a few girls. So if we were such a minor, you know, of the girls, we were one of the boys. Anyone that came over with the 45 group is one of the boys, because we were in the minority. Can you imagine there was 300 transports and we're 28 girls. The rest is all boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it feel like to be with all these boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was lovely. I mean, that, that feeling was really good. It was wonderful, I mean, to be together. The main thing is survival. At the time, we were still so very worried, thinking of, of all our-- look, wonderful was it we survived. But we also had our problems. We were very worried about finding family. And how would we carry on with our life and [SIGHS] I didn't have my mother to guide me. I didn't have my father. I didn't have my family. I always said if I had at least my brother who would be alive, he would have help me. It would have been all right. It was very, very traumatic, very traumatic times, and very, very hard. And we couldn't talk about it much. But we felt it all inside us. It was very hard in the beginning. That was very hard, very.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did, did any of the boys and girls get-- got actually married within the group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A couple got married. One got married and her husband died. And the other one, they're OK. They actually moved just now. They moved here. They live in, in, in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: But mostly it was more like brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Brothers and sisters. Most of the boys married really girls, English Jewish girls. And the girls also-- I mean, I would have definitely married one of our boys. But I was so ill with tuberculosis. I couldn't think of marriage in the beginning. If it wasn't that thing that he was so much after me. And I met him-- probably I would have not been so quickly married anyway. I would have gone with my sister to Israel and then something would have happened--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the other girls--

speaker: interviewee
text: --because I couldn't think of getting married, knowing I got tuberculosis. I had to go every month still for checkups.

speaker: interviewer
text: But many of the other girls got married in that period as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later. I was the first girl to get married as a matter of fact.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the boys, yes. Maybe some of the boys got married. But from the girls, definitely I was the first, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then did you take your husband to the Primrose Club as well or--

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to go sometimes in the beginning, yes. We used to go in the beginning with my first husband. Yeah, because I don't think it existed-- this didn't exist enough days. We were-- we were-- look, it was 17 years we were living here, then 10 years in the Italy. It's 27 years afterwards. There wasn't anything-- no sign of it anymore. When we were in Italy already it was not anymore. People were getting married, moving away. And some people went to live in Israel. Some people went to live in America. It was all changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the atmosphere in the Primrose Club, was it opened or closed? Oh, when-- at the time when we were youngsters and first went to the club, it was a haven for us. It was a place where we could meet-- somewhere to go. We had nowhere to go to. Did we have parents to look-- to go to? Did we have family? We had nobody. That was the place to go. That's why it was so clever when they did open such a place for us. Is there anyone in particular you remember of the madrichim or of the teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am so much out of contact. There was one. His name was Israel but I don't know his surname. And his wife-- they, they actually were madrichas in Windemere. She was such a pretty girl. And he's such a nice man-- such a handsome man. And they met as madrichas looking after us. They got married and [INAUDIBLE] people and afterwards I think they had about eight or nine children. Once, years ago, when I was still a girl, me and my friend, Rella, she took us to Madame Tussauds. This is the first time we ever went to see Madame Tussauds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to add or you think we haven't discussed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just I want to say that this terrible traumatic times were there and that never-- it's always with you, even though you try to push it back to back of your mind. But when it comes holidays, when there comes-- it's very, very-- you can never get over it. One thing I must say also when I came to England, we wanted very much to go to Israel. But for me, personally, it was the best thing that I came to England because in Israel, I could have not survived it because I was very ill. And the main thing was that I was taken out of this hell from-- to be sent England. And for me, Windermere, the lake, it was a paradise. And they put me on my feet.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the only-- the only regret which I have is that I, I-- we didn't grow up together having to be with my sister and her children and my children because they love me so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have got a niece who I adore, Rachel, Rachela. She is-- I tell you, most of the thing which are here come from her. I never buy any of these things. She always sends me from Israel. Coming from every-- that green item there, that, that little bowl, this-- whatever you see around here, she always-- this, with chocolates, full of chocolates. And when I come in Israel, she doesn't know what to do for me. And I have got a wonderful family in Israel. And my sister has always been to me like a mother because, because we were in such a way separated, that we never believed we would ever see other again. So she's-- she always wanted to do everything for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any message you want to give?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the message is that I love very much my sister and her children, Rachela, [? Hindala, ?] and Jacob and all the children and grandchildren. And we should have shalom in Israel. Please God, this is the main thing. And very often I miss them so much. I wish I could live in Israel, be with them near. But it's so difficult once you've made the roots here and you've got your own children. But I'm still hoping. I'm still hoping one day to be in Israel. This is my, my dream.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Jay, thank you very much for the interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please tell me who's on this photos?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my cousin, a Munter-- from the Munters. It was taken before the war. He was a lovely fellow, educated. He was in Lodz ghetto with us. And they made him the manager in a factory, and he didn't survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sigmund. Sigmund Munter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you possession of-- how did you get this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was here in England because we had family from the [INAUDIBLE] here in England. And one day, when I saw it, I said I must have this photo. And they have it to me. But I have no photos of my own family directly. I mean, this is my cousin. He's a Munter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Warta. That was in Warta. It looks like it's near the river somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please describe this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was taken in, in Windermere--

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: --this picture. In 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is on the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is Rella. And she-- I was always together with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where? Which one is Rella?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rella is to the right. And I am to the left. That's me, on the left. Or was it Windemere [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please tell me about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This photo was sent to me when I was in Windermere. The very first photo that I found out that my sister is alive. And it-- and I-- it was actually-- it's written in English on the other side. This photo was sent by the pilot, who sits to the left. When I finally got this photo, I didn't realize where my sister was. I thought maybe she's in England because it was in English. But the, the p-- pilot actually was looking for family. My sister never believed that she would ever find me alive. And when he found a Munter on the-- on the-- on the list in [NON-ENGLISH] he referred straight away to my sister. And when I-- I couldn't believe who the three men were. Well, my sister got married. To the right is her husband. And to the left is the man who was looking for, for our family survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is taken in Germany, in [? Luneburg. ?] This picture has been written to send to me by the pilot to show that there is a picture of my sister on, on this picture. This picture has been taken in actually in the sanatorium. These are the people I was together in the sanatorium. And I know each one here very well. We were only two girls. That's Rella, my best friend, to the left. And to the right, there is Sister Maria, who died a few years ago. But she was holding me there. And that is me in the middle, where she's holding me. The rest are all the boys who were with me in the sanatorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the sanatorium?

speaker: interviewee
text: The sanatorium was in Ashford, Kent. It was five miles away from the little town Ashford, from the town Ashford.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when exactly was the photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please describe this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in the sanatorium. And this is how we had-- we had our English lessons. We had a teacher which came. And we were sitting in the dining room, and on the table, as you can see, we were sitting around and learning English-- the English language. [NON-ENGLISH] Look, this has come off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Can we-- can you describe the photo please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taking in Kings Langley. That was when we were on a re-convalescent in this place. And these were girls who came over with the second transport, most of them. I am standing next to the lady in the black dress, who was our matron. I think she was a cook, actually. She was a cook. And holding her arm around her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Can you please tell me about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is the photo when I got married the first time to Kurt Wassing. It was a wonderful time when I met him and we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had wonderful children with-- wonderful children together.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken the 7th of November 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the synagogue. We actually got married in the [INAUDIBLE] synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please describe this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken in Hyde Park. It's actually a place where I met my husband. And here, we went out with my friend, Rella, who was leaving for Israel. And she actually went to Israel and lives in Tel Aviv.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the photo of taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken in 1949-- spring 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe this photo please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was when-- a time in my life when I really enjoyed life very much. I was happily married. We had a lovely holiday in the south of France with the children-- with my husband and children. And my father-in-law came as well. It was really lovely times.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] 1957 maybe. I don't know how old. You were about three and a half. 1957.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is who? Who is who, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me. In the middle, that's me and my two children. There is Denise and Michael. Denise is the older one. Denise is my daughter and my son Michael, to the left.
